clauses.name: Mutuality
clauses.requirement: The NDA must bind both parties symmetrically as disclosing and receiving parties. A both-sides Confidential Information DEFINITION ('disclosed by either party') does NOT by itself establish mutual obligations; require an operative confidentiality COVENANT that binds EACH party as Receiving Party. A covenant obligating only 'the Recipient' / 'the Receiving Party' to protect 'the Disclosing Party's' information is one-way, even when the definition is bilateral.
clauses.preferred_position: Mutual NDA: both parties act as Disclosing Party and Receiving Party, with symmetric confidentiality obligations.
clauses.check_trigger: One-way or non-mutual language that binds only one side, fixes one party as the only Receiving Party, or otherwise fails to make both parties disclosure and receipt parties.
clauses.redline_template: Each party may disclose Confidential Information under this Agreement, and each party acts as both a Disclosing Party and a Receiving Party with respect to Confidential Information it discloses or receives.
semantic_signals: each of the parties
either party may disclose
the parties may disclose confidential information to each other
each party acts as both disclosing party and receiving party
a mutuality title, recital, definition, or non-mutual label is only a weak cue unless operative confidentiality obligations are reciprocal
a both-sides definition (information disclosed by either party) is not by itself mutual obligations; the operative covenant must bind each party as receiving party
a covenant obligating only the recipient or receiving party to protect the disclosing party's information is one-way even when the definition is bilateral
one-way return, destroy, delete, certify, copy-control, or other administrative duties are not one-way confidentiality obligations by themselves
clauses.rationale: A one-way NDA creates asymmetric risk; Aspora's baseline is reciprocal confidentiality unless the matter is deliberately unilateral.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Look for operative language tying both parties to disclosing and receiving roles, not only isolated defined terms, titles, recitals, or non-mutual labels. Do not treat one-way return, destroy, delete, certify, copy-control, or other administrative duties as one-way confidentiality failures unless they are the operative confidentiality obligation.

rules.acceptable_position: Both parties can disclose and receive Confidential Information, and confidentiality obligations operate symmetrically.

pass_conditions.description: The contract clearly makes each party both a Disclosing Party and a Receiving Party, or otherwise creates reciprocal confidentiality obligations. Administrative one-way duties about returning, destroying, deleting, certifying, or controlling copies do not defeat a clear reciprocal confidentiality covenant.

fail_conditions.description: No operative language clearly makes confidentiality obligations mutual.
The contract fixes only one party as the Receiving Party or otherwise creates operative one-way confidentiality obligations.

review_triggers.description: Mutuality appears only in a title, recital, definition, or separated role language, and the operative confidentiality obligations are SILENT or UNCLEAR (not affirmatively one-way) about whether each party is bound reciprocally. This REVIEW trigger is for genuine uncertainty only: if the operative covenant is affirmatively one-way (it binds only the Recipient / Receiving Party to protect the Disclosing Party's information), that is the one_way_party_roles FAIL, not this review.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the operative language that creates reciprocal roles, or cite the one-way confidentiality language that fixes only one receiving party. Do not cite a title alone or a non-mutual/without-reciprocity phrase as evidence of mutuality.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the mutuality template to add or replace language so both parties can disclose and receive Confidential Information.

clauses.acceptable_language: Each party may disclose Confidential Information and each party acts as both a Disclosing Party and Receiving Party.

clauses.name: Confidential Information
clauses.requirement: Confidential Information must be broad and must cover BOTH the existence and terms of the Agreement AND the right of publicity. These two inclusions are non-negotiable: a definition that is otherwise broad but clearly OMITS the right of publicity, or clearly OMITS the existence and terms of the Agreement, is a FAIL (required_inclusions_missing) -- not a clean pass and not merely a review. Use review only when it is genuinely unclear whether a broad catch-all reaches the inclusion. Residual-knowledge and reverse-engineering carve-outs FAIL. Any other extra carve-out beyond the standard public-domain, prior-possession, lawful third-party source, and qualified independent-development set is flagged for human REVIEW -- it is neither a clean pass nor a fail. The protection must also stand on its own: the standard carve-outs/exclusions must not be cancelled or disapplied elsewhere in the document, the definition must not affirmatively include information the carve-outs exclude (publicly available, already known, or independently developed), and the agreement must not be subordinated to a separate external agreement (such as a Master Services Agreement) that is given overriding authority over these confidentiality terms.
clauses.preferred_position: Broad definition covering all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret and source code information, including the right of publicity and the existence and terms of the Agreement, whether marked or unmarked.
clauses.check_trigger: The definition is missing, too narrow, carves out core categories, clearly OMITS the right of publicity, clearly OMITS the existence and terms of the Agreement, or includes a residual-knowledge or reverse-engineering exclusion (FAIL). A clear omission of either required inclusion is a FAIL (required_inclusions_missing), not a review; reserve review only for the genuinely-unclear case where a broad catch-all might or might not reach the inclusion. An extra carve-out beyond the standard public-domain, prior-possession, lawful third-party source, and qualified independent-development set -- and which is NOT one of the failing residual/reverse-engineering ones -- is flagged for human REVIEW, not a fail and not a clean pass. Also trigger when a provision elsewhere cancels or disapplies the carve-outs/exclusions ('notwithstanding the foregoing, the exclusions shall not apply'), the definition affirmatively includes a normally-excluded category (poisoned definition), or the NDA is made subject to / incorporated into / subordinate to an external agreement that prevails or takes precedence over it.
clauses.redline_template: "Confidential Information" means any and all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret and source code information disclosed by either party, including the right of publicity and the existence and terms of this Agreement, whether disclosed orally, visually, electronically or in writing and whether or not marked confidential. Confidential Information does not include information that is public through no breach of this Agreement, already known without confidentiality obligation, received from a lawful third party without confidentiality obligation, or independently developed without use of or reference to Confidential Information.
clauses.standard_exclusions_template: Confidential Information does not include information that is public through no breach of this Agreement, already known without confidentiality obligation, received from a lawful third party without confidentiality obligation, or independently developed without use of or reference to Confidential Information.
semantic_signals: non-public information
proprietary information
information disclosed by either party
information whether oral written electronic or visual
residual knowledge or residuals excluded from Confidential Information
reverse-engineering exclusion or reverse-engineering usage right
independent-development exclusion without use/access/reference qualification
separate usage-right language allowing use, retention, exploitation, or independent development outside the exclusion list
clauses.rationale: The definition should catch the business material actually exchanged in an NDA workflow without adding carve-outs that weaken protection.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Anchor on the defined term, then inspect the same paragraph and nearby exclusion or usage-right language for category breadth, residuals, reverse engineering, and independent-development qualifications. Separately, check the definition for the two REQUIRED inclusions: the right of publicity AND the existence and terms of the Agreement. If the definition is broad but clearly does NOT cover the right of publicity, or clearly does NOT cover the existence and terms of the Agreement, that is a FAIL (required_inclusions_missing) -- do not soften a clear omission to review; cite the definition span and note which required inclusion is absent. Only route to review when a broad catch-all genuinely might or might not reach the inclusion. A negated prohibition such as 'may not reverse engineer' is not a reverse-engineering carve-out. Also read the WHOLE document for structural overrides of the protection: (a) a clause that cancels/disapplies the exclusions ('the exclusions shall not apply', 'are void', 'are of no effect'), (b) a definition that affirmatively INCLUDES public, already-known, or independently-developed information, and (c) an incorporation-by-reference / 'subject to' / 'subordinate to' clause giving a separate external agreement overriding authority that prevails in conflict. Any of these undermines the Confidential Information protection even when the definition paragraph itself reads normally.

rules.acceptable_position: Confidential Information is defined broadly enough to cover non-public business, financial, technical, customer, employee, supplier, pricing, market, product, proprietary, trade secret, and source code information, as well as the right of publicity and the existence and terms of the Agreement, with only standard carve-outs.

pass_conditions.description: The definition covers the material categories in the playbook -- including, expressly, the right of publicity and the existence and terms of the Agreement -- and its exclusion list contains EXCLUSIVELY the standard accepted carve-outs (public-domain, prior-possession, lawful third-party source, and independent-development qualified by no use of, access to, or reference to Confidential Information) and no others. A broad definition that omits the right of publicity or the existence and terms of the Agreement does not satisfy this condition. Any extra carve-out beyond the standard set also does NOT satisfy this condition: a residual-knowledge or reverse-engineering exclusion is a FAIL, and any other extra exclusion routes to REVIEW.

fail_conditions.description: The document does not define Confidential Information.
The definition omits important protected categories.

The definition or exclusion list excludes residual knowledge, residuals, reverse engineering, or a reverse-engineering right. Do not treat a negated prohibition on reverse engineering as an exclusion.

The Confidential Information definition omits a required inclusion -- the right of publicity, or the existence and terms of the Agreement -- both of which must be covered. A definition that is otherwise broad but clearly does NOT cover the right of publicity, or clearly does NOT cover the existence and terms of the Agreement, is a FAIL (these inclusions are non-negotiable), not a pass and not merely a review.

review_triggers.description: The definition or nearby exclusions are ambiguous enough that a human should confirm whether protection is materially narrowed.
The definition uses a common independent-development carve-out but does not clearly tie it to development without use of, access to, or reference to Confidential Information.
Separate usage-right language may create an extra residual-knowledge, reverse-engineering, or unqualified independent-development carve-out outside the exclusion list.

It is genuinely UNCLEAR whether the definition covers a required inclusion -- the existence and terms of the Agreement, or the right of publicity -- for example because a broad catch-all phrase ('any and all information', 'including without limitation') might or might not reach it. Use this REVIEW only for that ambiguity. If the definition clearly OMITS the right of publicity or the existence and terms of the Agreement, that is the required_inclusions_missing FAIL, not this review.

The exclusion list contains an extra carve-out beyond the standard set (public-domain, prior-possession, lawful third-party source, qualified independent-development) that is NOT a residual-knowledge or reverse-engineering exclusion -- for example a feedback/suggestions or other usage-right exclusion the disclosing party may freely use. An extra exclusion outside the approved standard set is neither a clean pass nor a fail; flag it for human review. Residual-knowledge and reverse-engineering exclusions are the exception and remain a FAIL.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the defined term paragraph and any nearby exclusion or usage-right language that supports the verdict. For a pass on independent development, cite the no-use/no-access/no-reference qualification.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the full definition template for missing or narrow definitions; use the standard exclusions template when only exclusions need correction.

clauses.acceptable_language: Confidential Information means any and all non-public business, financial, technical, customer, supplier, pricing, market, proprietary and trade secret information disclosed by either party, including the right of publicity and the existence and terms of the Agreement.

clauses.name: Governing Law
clauses.requirement: Governing law must be India, Delaware, England and Wales, DIFC, or Ontario, Canada, AND the dispute forum must be aligned to that governing law: the exclusive jurisdiction / venue / arbitration seat must name the same approved jurisdiction as the operative governing law. A forum that names a different or unapproved jurisdiction than the approved governing law is a defect that must be reconciled by aligning the forum to the approved governing law.
clauses.preferred_position: The governing law is one of the approved jurisdictions, preferably England and Wales unless the matter context supports another approved option.
clauses.check_trigger: FAIL when the operative governing-law clause names a clearly-identified jurisdiction outside the approved set (India, Delaware, England and Wales, DIFC, Ontario Canada). FAIL when no operative governing-law clause is found. REVIEW (not FAIL) only when the governing-law language is genuinely unclear, conditional, conflicting, incorporates a reference to another document, uses a placeholder or TBD marker, allows the parties to choose or vary the law later, or contains a secondary carve-out that creates ambiguity about which law controls. Do not return REVIEW when the jurisdiction is unambiguously named and simply unapproved.

law_phrases.Ontario, Canada: the Province of Ontario and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein

semantic_signals: this agreement is governed
agreement shall be construed in accordance with
laws applicable to this agreement
party incorporation or organization recitals are not governing-law clauses
a clearly-named unapproved operative jurisdiction such as California, New York, Singapore, France, or Germany must be FAIL not REVIEW
conditional override wording such as unless otherwise agreed weakens an otherwise approved law and needs review
secondary or carve-out governing-law language for disputes, remedies, IP, enforcement, or other sections needs review when it conflicts with or qualifies the main law
approved-law aliases such as English law, Indian law, DIFC law, Delaware law, Ontario law, Canadian law, or Province of Ontario may count only when they describe the agreement governing law
clauses.rationale: The preferred jurisdictions are the approved operating set for this workflow; anything else should be failed or reviewed depending on how clearly the governing law is named.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Use the operative governing-law sentence as evidence of the governing LAW, not the whole dispute-resolution section. Do not treat a party recital saying it is incorporated, organized, formed, registered, domiciled, or existing under the laws of a jurisdiction as the agreement governing law. When an approved governing law is followed by unless otherwise agreed, subject to another agreement, chosen by a party, or a secondary carve-out for disputes, remedies, IP, enforcement, or another topic, cite that qualifying span and return REVIEW rather than pass. FAIL (not REVIEW) when the operative governing-law sentence names a clearly-identified unapproved jurisdiction such as California, New York, Singapore, France, Germany, or any other jurisdiction not in the approved list — reserve REVIEW for genuinely uncertain or conditional cases, not clearly-named wrong jurisdictions. Approved-law aliases count only when they modify the agreement governing-law clause. A court-venue, exclusive-jurisdiction, or arbitration-seat reference is NOT evidence of the governing LAW itself, and an approved jurisdiction appearing only in addresses, party descriptions, court venue, tax, employment, or regulatory references does not establish the governing law. HOWEVER, when the governing law is approved but the exclusive jurisdiction / venue / arbitration seat names a DIFFERENT or unapproved jurisdiction, you MUST cite that venue/forum span as evidence of a forum-alignment defect (the forum-does-not-match-governing-law FAIL) — here the venue text is the load-bearing evidence, not a thing to ignore. For failures, cite the operative unapproved governing-law sentence, or the conflicting forum/venue span for a forum-alignment defect; for missing, explain that no operative governing-law sentence was found.

rules.acceptable_position: The governing law is one of the approved jurisdictions in the playbook, with England and Wales as the default preferred option unless matter context supports another approved jurisdiction.

pass_conditions.description: The operative governing-law clause for this Agreement names India, Delaware, England and Wales, DIFC, or Ontario, Canada, including clear aliases such as English law, Indian law, DIFC law, Delaware law, Ontario law, or Canadian law, and is not qualified by a later override or competing governing-law carve-out.

fail_conditions.description: No operative governing-law clause is found. Party incorporation, organization, formation, registration, domicile, address, court venue, tax, employment, or regulatory-law references do not establish the governing LAW by themselves. (A court-venue / exclusive-jurisdiction reference is excluded here only as evidence of the governing law; when the governing law IS approved, that same venue text is still the evidence for a forum-alignment defect — see forum_does_not_match_governing_law.)

The operative governing-law clause names a clearly-identified jurisdiction outside the approved playbook list (e.g. California, New York, Singapore, France, Germany, or any other non-approved jurisdiction). This is a FAIL, not a REVIEW — do not soften a clearly-named unapproved jurisdiction to review. Do not fail merely because a party is incorporated or organized under an unapproved jurisdiction if the agreement itself is governed by an approved law. Only use REVIEW when the governing-law language is genuinely unclear, conditional, or conflicting.

The operative governing-law jurisdiction is approved, but the exclusive jurisdiction / venue / arbitration seat names a different or unapproved jurisdiction; the forum must be aligned to the approved governing law. Cite the conflicting forum/venue span. The correct fix is to align the forum to the approved governing law (keep the approved law; move the courts/seat to that same jurisdiction), NOT to switch the governing law to the unapproved forum's jurisdiction.

review_triggers.description: The governing-law language is unclear, conditional, conflicting, heading-only, incorporated by reference, subject to later agreement, chosen by a party, or mixed with secondary governing-law carve-outs for disputes, remedies, IP, enforcement, or other sections. REVIEW is reserved for genuine uncertainty — do NOT use REVIEW for a clearly-named jurisdiction that is simply unapproved; that must be FAIL.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the operative governing-law sentence. If the issue is conditional or secondary governing-law language, cite the qualifying or conflicting span too. If missing, explain that no operative governing-law sentence was found. Do not cite a party-incorporation recital, address, venue-only, tax, employment, or regulatory reference as governing-law evidence.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use one of the approved jurisdiction options. Default to England and Wales unless another approved option is selected.

clauses.acceptable_language: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of England and Wales, India, Delaware, the DIFC, or the Province of Ontario and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein.

clauses.name: Term and Survival
clauses.requirement: The NDA term and ordinary confidentiality survival must be fixed at up to five years.
clauses.preferred_position: Ordinary confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to five years. Narrow trade-secret, legal/regulatory, and data-protection obligations may survive for as long as the protected status or law requires.
clauses.check_trigger: Ordinary confidentiality is perpetual, indefinite, relationship-based, tied to information remaining confidential, longer than five years, or missing a clear fixed term or survival period.
clauses.redline_template: The confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to {max_term_years_label}, except that trade secrets, legally required confidentiality, and personal data protected by data-protection law survive for as long as the protected status or law requires.
semantic_signals: confidentiality obligations continue
obligations remain binding
obligations remain in force
after termination
ordinary confidentiality obligations or the agreement term are subject to the five-year cap
audit, tax, payment, warranty, indemnity, insurance, employment, and records-retention periods are duration decoys unless they set the confidentiality term
long or indefinite survival can pass only when scoped to trade secrets, legal obligations, regulatory duties, personal data, or data-protection obligations
clauses.rationale: Ordinary confidentiality should be time-limited; indefinite language is only acceptable for narrow trade-secret or legal exceptions.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Prefer paragraphs containing duration language plus a year count or indefinite survival wording. Count only durations tied to the agreement term or ordinary confidentiality obligations; ignore audit, tax, payment, warranty, indemnity, insurance, employment, books-and-records, and records-retention periods unless they expressly set confidentiality survival. Long or indefinite survival is acceptable only when scoped to trade secrets, legal obligations, regulatory duties, personal data, or data-protection obligations; the same long period for ordinary confidentiality fails.

rules.acceptable_position: Ordinary confidentiality obligations and the agreement term have a fixed duration of up to five years. Narrow trade-secret, legal, regulatory, personal-data, or data-protection carve-outs may survive longer or indefinitely only to the extent the protected status or law requires.

pass_conditions.description: The agreement term or ordinary confidentiality survival period is fixed and does not exceed five years. Longer or indefinite language is acceptable only when it is expressly scoped to trade secrets, legal obligations, regulatory duties, personal data, or data-protection obligations.

fail_conditions.description: No clear term or ordinary confidentiality survival period is found.

Ordinary confidentiality survival is indefinite, perpetual, relationship-based, tied to information remaining confidential, or longer than five years. Do not fail merely because an unrelated audit, tax, payment, warranty, indemnity, insurance, employment, or records-retention period exceeds five years.

review_triggers.description: The term language is ambiguous, cross-referenced, unresolved, or unclear about whether the long survival applies only to accepted carve-outs rather than ordinary confidentiality.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the term or survival paragraph, including the duration or indefinite wording. If missing, explain that no clear term or survival period was found. Do not cite unrelated audit, tax, payment, warranty, indemnity, insurance, employment, or records-retention periods as evidence of ordinary confidentiality duration.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the survival template with the playbook maximum term and narrow longer-survival carve-outs.

indefinite_terms: for so long as the information remains confidential
until it ceases to have commercial value
until it ceases to have value
for so long as it retains commercial value
as long as it remains secret
clauses.acceptable_language: The confidentiality obligations survive for a fixed period of up to five years, except for trade secrets or legal obligations that require a longer period.

clauses.name: Non-Circumvention
clauses.requirement: The NDA must not include non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restrictions, substitute-purpose exclusivity, IP assignment, penalty/liquidated-damages, auto-renewal lock, or similar prohibited business positions.
clauses.preferred_position: The NDA should not restrict direct dealings, introduced contacts, hiring/solicitation, competition, substitute transactions, exclusivity, ownership of IP, damages remedies, renewal/termination freedom, or similar commercial conduct beyond confidentiality.
clauses.check_trigger: Any non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restriction, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, exclusivity/exclusive-dealing, IP-assignment, penalty/liquidated/punitive-damages, evergreen auto-renewal, or no-termination lock appears in the NDA.
semantic_signals: operative restriction on direct or indirect dealings with introduced contacts, counterparties, customers, investors, suppliers, employees, or business opportunities
bypassing, avoiding, circumventing, cutting out, interfering with, inducing, recruiting, hiring, poaching, or otherwise diverting introduced parties away from the disclosing party
exclusive dealing, substitute-purpose, competing-product, competing-service, or similar restraint that uses confidential information or introductions to block ordinary commercial freedom
unlisted verbs can still be prohibited when the sentence creates an operative business restraint beyond confidentiality
a nearby freedom-preserving sentence does not cure a separate operative restraint; the genuine co-located prohibition controls
negated references, disclaimers, and freedom-preserving carve-outs are not prohibited restrictions
references to lawful circumvention of laws, sanctions, or regulatory obligations are not non-circumvention restrictions
non-compete or competition restraints are prohibited even if phrased as use of Confidential Information or introduced opportunities
non-solicitation includes unhyphenated and interposed wording such as shall not directly or indirectly solicit or hire introduced employees, consultants, customers, or suppliers
IP assignment language such as hereby assigns all right title and interest is prohibited in an NDA unless it is only a narrow confidentiality ownership acknowledgement
liquidated damages, penalty, punitive damages, or preset penalty amounts are prohibited legal positions
auto-renewal, evergreen, exclusive renewal, or may-not-terminate locks are prohibited business positions
clauses.rationale: Non-circumvention and substitute-purpose restrictions turn the NDA into a broader commercial restraint.
clauses.evidence_guidance: For failures, cite the exact operative restriction that limits dealings, introduced contacts, substitute transactions, hiring/recruiting/poaching, competition, interference, exclusivity, IP ownership, damages remedies, renewal, or termination freedom. For review, cite the ambiguous span. Do not cite a negated reference, freedom-preserving carve-out, or lawful/regulatory circumvention reference as a prohibited restriction.

rules.acceptable_position: The NDA does not impose non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restrictions, substitute-purpose exclusivity, IP assignment, liquidated/punitive damages, auto-renewal locks, or similar commercial restraints beyond confidentiality. It may expressly preserve lawful dealings or say no non-circumvention obligation is created.

pass_conditions.description: No operative non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restriction, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, hiring/recruiting/poaching, interference, competition, exclusivity, IP-assignment, penalty/liquidated/punitive damages, auto-renewal, evergreen, or no-termination lock appears. Negated references, lawful-circumvention references, and freedom-preserving carve-outs pass when they do not impose the restriction.

fail_conditions.description: The document includes an operative non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restriction, substitute-purpose, direct-dealing, bypassing, hiring/recruiting/poaching, interference, competition, exclusivity, IP-assignment, penalty/liquidated/punitive damages, auto-renewal, evergreen, no-termination lock, or similar prohibited position. The exact verb is not controlling; fail any clear business restraint beyond confidentiality, including when it appears near freedom-preserving language.

review_triggers.description: The language mentions a prohibited-position concept, but the scope, polarity, actor, condition, legal effect, or relationship to confidentiality is ambiguous enough that a human should decide.

evidence_requirements.guidance: For failures, cite the exact operative restriction. For review, cite the ambiguous wording. For passes, absence of prohibited language, a negated reference, a lawful-circumvention reference, or a freedom-preserving carve-out may be enough.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Remove the prohibited restraint rather than replacing it with another commercial restriction.

clauses.acceptable_language: No non-circumvention, non-compete, introduced-party non-solicit or hiring restriction, substitute-purpose exclusivity, IP assignment, liquidated/punitive damages, auto-renewal lock, or similar prohibited business restraint should appear in the NDA. Acceptable language may include freedom-preserving carve-outs saying that nothing restricts a party from dealing with third parties, that no non-circumvention obligation is created, that IP ownership is unchanged, or that parties must not circumvent applicable laws or regulatory obligations.

prohibited_position_patterns.label: non_compete
prohibited_position_patterns.pattern: non-?compete|shall not (?:directly or indirectly )?(?:compete|engage in any business that competes)|competing business

prohibited_position_patterns.label: non_circumvention
prohibited_position_patterns.pattern: non-?circumvent|shall not circumvent|circumvent or bypass|bypass the disclosing party|\bdeal\s+directly\b|introduced\s+part

prohibited_position_patterns.label: exclusivity
prohibited_position_patterns.pattern: sole and exclusive|deal exclusively|exclusive right to|exclusive dealing|exclusivity (?:obligation|arrangement|provision)

prohibited_position_patterns.label: ip_assignment
prohibited_position_patterns.pattern: hereby assigns?\b|assignment of (?:all )?intellectual property|all (?:right,? )?title and interest in

clauses.name: Signatures
clauses.requirement: The execution block must show both parties, signatory titles, and a date.
clauses.preferred_position: Both parties have complete execution blocks showing legal entity name, authorised signatory, title or capacity, and date.
clauses.check_trigger: The execution block is missing, one party block is absent, or signatory name, title/capacity, or date markers are incomplete.
semantic_signals: executed by
signed for and on behalf of
authorised signatory
name title date
clauses.rationale: A complete execution block reduces signing ambiguity and supports clean downstream storage.
clauses.evidence_guidance: Look for both parties' execution blocks with signatory, title or capacity, and date markers.

rules.acceptable_position: Both parties have complete execution blocks showing legal entity name, authorised signatory, title or capacity, and date.

pass_conditions.description: Execution blocks show both parties, signatory markers, titles or capacities, and dates.

fail_conditions.description: No usable execution block is found.
One or both execution blocks are present but missing party, signatory, title, or date markers.

review_triggers.description: Signature material is present but it is unclear whether both parties and required markers are complete.

evidence_requirements.guidance: Cite the execution block when present. If missing, explain that no complete execution block was found.

redline_guidance.drafting_note: Use the execution-block template when signatures are missing or materially incomplete.

clauses.redline_template:
For [Party 1 legal name]
By:
Name:
Title:
Date:

For [Party 2 legal name]
By:
Name:
Title:
Date:
clauses.acceptable_language: Each party should have an execution block showing the legal entity name, authorised signatory, title, and date.
